$SYK Q1 2026 earnings: Cyberattack Derails Q1, But Full-Year Targets Defy Gravity  Stryker’s multi-year streak of double-digit growth hit a brick wall in Q1 following a severe cyberattack in March. The disruption halted momentum instantly: organic revenue growth decelerated to 2.4% (from 11.0% in 25Q4). Because short-term costs are largely fixed, the sudden volume drop crushed operating leverage. Adjusted EPS fell 8.5% YoY to $2.60, and adjusted operating margins compressed by 180 basis points. Despite the Q1 crater, management maintained its aggressive FY26 guidance (8.0-9.5% organic growth). This signals absolute confidence that orders were delayed, not permanently lost. However, it mathematically requires a massive, flawless acceleration in the remaining three quarters to hit the targets.  Full article with charts - link in bio  🐂 𝗕𝘂𝗹𝗹 𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝗿𝘂𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁: The cyber incident is a one-time operational shock. By maintaining full-year guidance, management is confirming that the capital order book and underlying hospital demand remain fully intact. 𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗣𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗼𝘀 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁: Despite the chaos, the Vascular and Trauma & Extremities segments grew 10.5% and 6.8% organically, proving that high-priority clinical procedures powered through the IT disruptions.  🐻 𝗕𝗲𝗮𝗿 𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗲𝗽 𝗛𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝘁𝗼 𝗖𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗯: Because Q1 fell so far behind, Q2-Q4 must now average roughly 11% organic growth and nearly 15% EPS growth. Any further macro friction or lingering remediation costs will cause a guidance miss. 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗶𝗻 𝗦𝗾𝘂𝗲𝗲𝘇𝗲: Adjusted gross margin collapsed by 190 basis points YoY. Remediating the cyber incident while fighting lingering tariff costs will keep the P&L under intense pressure.  ⚖️ 𝗩𝗲𝗿𝗱𝗶𝗰𝘁 ⚪ Neutral. The maintained guidance is a massive show of confidence, but the math is unforgiving. Stryker needs a perfect execution record for the rest of 2026 to fill the Q1 hole.  — • — • —  𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗺𝗲𝘀  New: 🔴🔴 𝗦𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗶𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 The sudden volume shock destroyed Stryker's operating leverage. Adjusted Gross Margin fell a staggering 190 basis points (from 65.5% in 25Q1 to 63.6% in 26Q1), and Adjusted Operating Margin dropped 180 bps to 21.1%. With fixed expenses staying high, the delayed revenue flowed directly to the bottom line as losses. Reversing this trend requires immediate volume recovery in Q2.  New: 🔴 𝗠𝗲𝗱𝗦𝘂𝗿𝗴 𝗚𝗿𝗼𝘄𝘁𝗵 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘀 Management claims 'underlying business momentum remains strong,' but the segment data contradicts this optimism. The historically robust MedSurg division was crippled: Medical segment organic sales contracted 6.9%, and Endoscopy declined 1.2%. The outsized hit to these capital-heavy divisions suggests their sales and deployment motions are highly vulnerable to IT and billing outages.  🟢 𝗩𝗮𝘀𝗰𝘂𝗹𝗮𝗿 𝗦𝗲𝗴𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵 Vascular was the standout performer, growing 27.5% reported and 10.5% organically. Boosted by the 2024 Inari acquisition, this segment successfully insulated itself from the broader Q1 IT disruptions, validating Stryker's strategic push into the high-growth peripheral vascular market.  New: 🟢 𝗢𝗿𝘁𝗵𝗼 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 Stryker reorganized its reporting, combining Mako robotics, enabling technologies, and power tools into the new 'Ortho Tech' unit. This segment grew 6.5% organically to $646M. Centralizing hardware and software simplifies the customer pitch and strengthens Stryker's grip on integrated Ambulatory Surgery Center (ASC) deployments.  🟢 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝘂𝗺𝗮 & 𝗘𝘅𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗻𝘀 𝗠𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘂𝗺 Despite the Q1 chaos, Trauma & Extremities posted a solid 6.8% organic growth ($1.03B). This business has been consistently taking market share, driven by recent platform launches like the Pangea plating system, which continues to see robust adoption regardless of broader corporate headwinds.  ⚪ 𝗣𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗿𝗼 & 𝗧𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗳𝗳 𝗣𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀 While the cyberattack dominated the quarter, underlying macro pressures remain. Stryker absorbed massive tariff headwinds (~$400M) in 2025. The 190 bps drop in Q1 gross margin suggests supply chain costs and geopolitical trade friction are still squeezing profitability heavily, even before cyber-remediation costs are tallied.  — • — • —  𝗢𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗞𝗣𝗜𝘀  𝗢𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗵 𝗙𝗹𝗼𝘄: $𝟱𝟴𝟭 𝗺𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻 Operating cash flow provided limited buffer in Q1. Combined with $1.0B used to pay down debt, total cash and equivalents dropped significantly from $4.01B at year-end to $2.88B. The balance sheet remains strong, but Q1 was a highly cash-consumptive quarter.  𝗦𝗽𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝗥𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘂𝗲: $𝟮 𝗺𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻 Reported revenue for Spinal Implants essentially zeroed out (down 98.9% YoY from $166M). This finalizes the divestiture transition of the U.S. Spinal Implants business, which mathematically dragged down reported Orthopaedics growth to +0.1%, masking the underlying +4.1% organic growth in the remaining core portfolio.  — • — • —  𝗚𝘂𝗶𝗱𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲  𝗙𝗬𝟮𝟲 𝗢𝗿𝗴𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗰 𝗡𝗲𝘁 𝗦𝗮𝗹𝗲𝘀: 𝟴.𝟬% - 𝟵.𝟱% Accelerating. With Q1 organic growth coming in at just 2.4%, hitting the 8.75% midpoint implies a steep acceleration to roughly 10.8% average organic growth for Q2-Q4. Management explicitly assumes the cyber-delayed orders will be rapidly fulfilled.  𝗙𝗬𝟮𝟲 𝗔𝗱𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗘𝗣𝗦: $𝟭𝟰.𝟵𝟬 - $𝟭𝟱.𝟭𝟬 Accelerating. The $15.00 midpoint implies roughly 10% YoY growth for the full year. Given the 8.5% YoY contraction in Q1, EPS must surge by roughly 15% YoY in the remaining three quarters, requiring flawless execution and rapid margin recovery.  — • — • —  𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗤𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀  𝗥𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘂𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝗰𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 How much of the Q1 revenue shortfall do you estimate is permanently lost to competitors versus simply delayed into the Q2 and Q3 order books?  𝗠𝗲𝗱𝗦𝘂𝗿𝗴 𝗩𝘂𝗹𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 What specific dynamics caused the Medical and Endoscopy divisions to contract organically, while Orthopaedics maintained growth through the cyber incident?  𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗖𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗖𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀 What are the expected ongoing costs associated with IT remediation and security upgrades, and are they fully factored into the maintained EPS guidance? 🔄 MITRE has released the scheduled ATT&CK Matrix v19 update.   It’s a major release that significantly reshapes the familiar Enterprise matrix. The headline change is the long-awaited split of the Defense Evasion tactic.  Defense Evasion has been divided into two tactics and then deprecated.  1. Stealth (TA0005) — behavior where an attacker hides and attempts to blend malicious activity into “normal” operations. Security tools keep running but fail to detect the threat.  2. Defense Impairment (TA0112) — behavior aimed at disrupting security mechanisms: disabling, shutting down, altering or deleting logs, modifying logging processes, impacting MFA, and so on.  The split is based on attacker intent. Some techniques appear in both tactics since real-world actions aren’t always clear-cut.  The most visible structural change is “T1562: Impair Defenses revoked.” T1562, T1562.001, and T1562.006 have been merged into a new parent technique, T1685: Disable or Modify Tools, while the remaining sub-techniques have been reassigned new IDs.  To assist migration, MITRE published a crosswalk in JSON -> (attack.mitre.org/docs/subtec…) and CSV -> (attack.mitre.org/docs/subtec…).  AI and Social Engineering New techniques have been added to reflect current realities:  T1682: Query Public AI Services — using public AI services for reconnaissance and attack planning. T1683: Generate Content with sub-techniques “Written Content” and “Audio-Visual Content” — content generation (manually, via intermediaries, or with AI assistance). T1684: Social Engineering — a new parent technique covering manipulation through any communication channel (email, voice, helpdesk, messengers). Impersonation and Email Spoofing are now its sub-techniques.  MITRE’s approach focuses on behavior rather than specific tools. AI makes attacks faster and cheaper, but the underlying actions remain the same.  Other Domains  ICS — sub-techniques have finally arrived. Five parent techniques were restructured: Modify Firmware, Block Communications, Remote System Discovery, Program Download, and the new Insecure Credentials. Mobile — Detection Strategies are now included here as well. Each strategy is vendor-agnostic, with separate analytics for Android and iOS. ブラジルのDDoS対策企業の内部が侵害され、同社インフラが大規模攻撃の踏み台として悪用されていた疑いが浮上した。漏えいした内部データから、数年にわたり国内ISPを標的とした攻撃基盤が運用されていた実態が明らかになった。  問題の企業はHuge Networksで、公開されたファイルにはPython製マルウェアやCEOのSSH秘密鍵が含まれていた。攻撃者はこれらを利用し、TP-Link製ルーター「Archer AX21」の脆弱性CVE-2023-1389を悪用して機器を大量感染させ、Mirai系ボットネットを構築していた。  このボットネットはDNSリフレクションと増幅攻撃を組み合わせ、小規模ISPに対して短時間かつ高強度のDDoSを繰り返していた。攻撃はブラジル国内IPに限定され、複数プロセスで効率的に実行されていた点も特徴だ。  CEOのErick Nascimento氏は関与を否定し、2026年1月に発生した侵害によりSSH鍵が漏えいし、不正利用された可能性を示唆している。現在は外部のフォレンジック企業が調査中で、競合企業による工作との見方も主張している。  krebsonsecurity.com/2026/04/…

QilinランサムウェアによるRDP横展開先の偵察手口として、防御側がDFIR（インシデント調査）で常用するWindowsイベントログを攻撃側がそのまま偵察に流用していた事例が報告されています。具体的には、PowerShellでEvent-ID-1149（RDP接続関連のオペレーショナルログ）を一括抽出することで、ユーザ名・ドメイン・接続元が揃い、横展開先の選定材料になると説明されています。  このログはSecurityチャネル外に置かれており、SIEMの既定収集から外れている環境が多いとされ、偵察行為自体が可視化されにくい構図が指摘されています。手口の構成要素自体は既知の調査テクニックの裏返しですが、Qilinの実侵害環境で観測された具体例として整理されたことが今回の報告の中心です。  【要点の整理】  ・不正なScreenConnect（リモート管理ツール）経由でPowerShellスクリプトが転送され、RDP接続履歴用のオペレーショナルログからEvent ID 1149を抽出し、横展開先候補を列挙する流れ  ・Event ID 1149はSecurityチャネル外のため、SIEMの既定収集対象に入っていない環境が多く、攻撃側の参照行為が可視化されにくいとの指摘  ・検知観点として、PowerShellからRDP関連オペレーショナルログへのアクセス、およびScriptBlock Logging（PowerShell実行内容の記録機能）でのGet-WinEvent系コマンドの可視化が挙げられている形  ・Qilinの規模感としては、被害主張・リークサイト掲載ベースで月40件超、累計700件超、最も勢いのある攻撃グループの一つ  報告では、防御側のDFIR用ログが攻撃側の偵察素材として参照され得る具体例として整理されています。  詳細は以下を参照： cybersecuritynews.com/qilin-…

sample_tweets.text: 🇮🇱 🚨 IMCO Group Targeted: Alleged 30TB Data Breach Claimed on Cybercrime Forum  A threat actor operating under the alias “Cyber_Isnaad_Front” claims to have breached the Israeli-based IMCO Group, alleging full compromise of the company’s network infrastructure. According to the post, approximately 30TB of data was exfiltrated, with around 10TB described as highly sensitive, particularly related to military and defense sectors. The actor states that the dataset includes production plans, defense-related components, contracts with major organizations, internal communications, and sensitive operational data. Additionally, the leak is claimed to contain employee PII, internal meeting materials, and even CCTV footage from facilities. The dataset is reportedly being offered for sale at $500,000.  #DataBreach #DarkWeb #Israel #ThreatIntelligence

🚨 Alleged Sale of cPanel Information Disclosure Vulnerability  A threat actor known as "NormalLeVrai" is selling an unpatched cPanel information disclosure vulnerability for $1,000. The vulnerability reportedly exposes website login credentials including panel/site links, usernames, and passwords across 13,522 cPanel instances spanning 94 countries.  #cPanel #Vulnerability #ThreatIntelligence #DarkWeb

🚨 Fraud-as-a-Service Campaign Targets Domain Suspension via Registrar Abuse and Legal Takedown Channels 🚨  A threat actor known as “convince” is advertising a Fraud-as-a-Service offering on underground forums, claiming the ability to forcibly suspend domains across major TLDs such as .com, .net, .org, .io, and .ai by abusing registrar abuse-reporting and legal takedown workflows. The service is marketed as a “Private Domain Suspension Method | Registrar Exploit 2026” and is positioned as a non-technical attack relying heavily on social engineering and process manipulation.  The offering is structured in two tiers, one providing a direct takedown service where the actor claims domains can be taken offline within 24 hours, and another offering a full methodology kit that teaches buyers how to replicate the process independently using prepared templates and submission channels.  According to the listing, the method focuses on bypassing frontline support and reaching registrar compliance or legal teams with forged or manipulated documentation, aiming to trigger actions such as domain suspension (clientHold status). The actor promotes the service for use against a wide range of targets, including competitors, journalists, researchers, and businesses.  Key risks highlighted by this activity include the ability to target high-value domains across multiple TLDs, the abuse of trust-based legal and compliance workflows, and the scalability of the method through resale of the toolkit.  #CyberCrime #FraudAsAService #DomainSecurity #ThreatIntelligence

🇺🇿 🚨 Alleged Breach of Uzbekistan Government Systems – Cybersecurity Center Staff Data Leaked  A threat actor claims to have breached internal systems of Uzbekistan’s national cybersecurity and intelligence infrastructure, including the Cybersecurity Center of Uzbekistan and the State Security Service of Uzbekistan. As proof, the actor has released a sample dataset containing details of 100 employees from the Cybersecurity Center, including names, positions, departments, and partially masked PINFL identifiers. The actor alleges possession of significantly larger and more sensitive datasets, including full staff databases of intelligence personnel, and is demanding $200,000 to prevent further disclosure.  #DataBreach #Uzbekistan #ThreatIntelligence #DarkWeb

🚨LAPSUS$ Revives Insider Recruitment Campaign Targeting Telecom, Tech, and AI Firms 🚨  The threat actor group LAPSUS$ has resurfaced with a renewed insider recruitment campaign, actively seeking employees from major telecom, technology, and AI organizations. The group is offering financial incentives in exchange for internal access, specifically targeting VPN/VDI credentials, Citrix sessions, remote access tools such as AnyDesk, or any foothold into corporate networks. Unlike traditional data breach operations, the campaign explicitly emphasizes gaining initial access through insiders rather than purchasing datasets. Targeted sectors reportedly include telecom providers, large software and gaming companies, AI firms, and call center/BPO environments.  #LAPSUS #InsiderThreat #CyberSecurity #ThreatIntelligence

🚨 Polymarket[.]com Allegedly Breached – 10M+ Records Leaked  A threat actor using the alias “xorcat” claims to have leaked over 10 million records from polymarket[.]com by exploiting multiple vulnerabilities, including unauthenticated API endpoints, a CORS misconfiguration, CVE-2025-62718 (Axios SSRF), and CVE-2024-51479 (Next.js authentication bypass). ‼️fast16: The Cyberweapon Hidden for 21 Years  SentinelLabs uncovered fast16, a previously undocumented cyber sabotage framework dating back to 2005, at least five years before Stuxnet. Unlike typical malware, fast16 was not built to steal data or visibly destroy systems. Its purpose was to silently corrupt high-precision engineering and scientific calculations, making software produce believable but deliberately wrong results.  The malware used a carrier called svcmgmt.exe, which embedded a Lua 5.0 virtual machine and could run as a Windows service, execute encrypted payloads, or spread across Windows 2000/XP networks using weak or default admin credentials. Its main sabotage component, fast16.sys, was a boot-level kernel driver that intercepted executable files compiled with the Intel C/C++ compiler and used 101 patching rules to alter floating-point calculations in memory while leaving files on disk unchanged.  SentinelLabs linked the patching logic to possible targets including LS-DYNA 970, PKPM, and MOHID, software used for explosions, structural engineering, seismic analysis, hydrodynamics, and other sensitive simulations. This could have impacted nuclear research, engineering design, or strategic infrastructure projects. A reference to fast16 also appeared in the ShadowBrokers 2017 leak of alleged NSA Equation Group materials, suggesting a possible state-backed or Equation Group/NSA connection, though attribution remains unconfirmed.  The malware stayed nearly invisible for years: svcmgmt.exe was uploaded to VirusTotal in 2016 and was detected by only 1 out of roughly 70 antivirus engines.  Source: sentinelone.com/labs/fast16-…

Imagine a 19-year-old scrolling TikTok. She watches a creator list five "signs you have undiagnosed anxiety." She recognizes three in herself. By the end of the week, she's describing herself as anxious to her friends. A month later, she's avoiding situations she used to handle fine.  What went wrong?  In a new paper by my PhD student Dasha Sandra, titled "Why mental health awareness can harm: Converging explanations for a societal problem", we argue that well-meaning mental health awareness can backfire, and we identify how. Four separate literatures (concept creep, nocebo effects, prevalence inflation, and illness self-labeling) have been circling the same problem from different angles. We show they converge on three mechanisms:  1.Awareness lowers the threshold for what counts as a disorder. 2. It trains people to scan their inner lives for symptoms and reinterpret normal distress as pathology. 3. Once someone adopts an illness identity, they behave in ways that confirm and deepen it.  The evidence is wide. Learning that loneliness is harmful makes solitude feel worse. Learning that stress is harmful worsens well-being and performance. Awareness videos about fake conditions like "wind turbine syndrome" produce real headaches. Trigger warnings raise anticipatory anxiety without reducing distress.  This does not mean awareness should stop. It means awareness can have unintended consequences, including manufacturing the suffering it tries to prevent. Inoculating people against these mechanisms works, and we already have evidence it does.  Link to paper: michael-inzlicht.squarespace…

This guy is in a lot of legal trouble. If the slide blew (I don’t think it did) I hope Delta sues him, too.  You hear the Captain on the PA system. The pilots are not going to come out when people start acting this way. We are now locked down & assume there may be other threats; this could be a coordinated distraction to take control of the cockpit.  Any kind of disruption like this is taken very seriously & we default to safety mode. The police are going to meet the jet. Now, here is what you might need to know…  If the Flight Attendants ask for help, get up & help them. They are trained to defuse these situations as much as possible, but can only do so much. When you are seated, the Flight Attendants will look around for passengers that might be able to help if things get physical. Do NOT do this on your own initiative…wait for the FAs to ask, then follow their instructions. They know what equipment is on the jet & where to put these people. Our security departments spend a lot of time thinking about this stuff & giving us training for it.  As much as I’d love to come storming out of the cockpit with the crash axe in hand, the cockpit door will not be opened in these circumstances.

We didn't know how an actor was using EV Certificates issued to Lenovo and others.  We now do.  From DigiCert's incident report:  "the threat actor used a compromised analyst endpoint to access  DigiCert's internal support portal. The threat actor used a limited  function within the customer-support portal which allows authenticated  DigiCert support analysts to access customer accounts from the  customer's perspective to facilitate support tasks. The threat actor  was able to use this function to access initialization codes for orders  that were approved but pending delivery for EV Code Signing certificate orders across a finite set of customer accounts."   "Possession of the initialization code, combined with an approved order, is functionally sufficient to generate and retrieve the corresponding  certificate."  The full report can be found here and explains the incident in great detail: bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bu…  The report mentions "Where we got lucky: A community member involved in security research reported the evolving  pattern of misused certificates and engaged in dialogue with our support team. Without that report, the undetected compromise of ENDPOINT2 and the associated mis-issuance might have remained undiscovered for a  longer period."   Special thanks goes to the regular contributors to the Cert Graveyard; @g0njxa , @malwrhunterteam , and others.   Also special thanks to DigiCert: this report has a high level of transparency, which is warranted, and also well executed.
